It's, so they basically, they have a treadmill station and then they have a rower station and then they have some sort of like resistance based station and they rotate you through those stations and then everyone's hooked up to a heart rate monitor and they put it up on the screen so it's like some fun gamification or whatever and they can track different heart rate zones that you're in and they want you in what's called an orange zone, so it's Orange Theory which is a particular heart rate zone about 70% of your maximum which is supposedly, some people disagree like myself, but that's where you burn the most fat is in this zone so they try and get you into the orange zone as often as possible. And I'm sure there's some other particulars but that's kind of the gist. Yeah. Yeah, yeah, for sure. I'm very similar to you as well. I want to tone, muscle tone. Cool. I love to educate and talk about all this stuff so that's why I'll open the door with questions because I love answering that. But with muscle gain, so a male who's never lifted weights ever jumps into all weight lifting only can gain about 25 pounds of muscle in a year. So, in 12 months. Females gain about 40% of that because you have 40% of the androgens that a male has which is the anabolic hormones that help build muscle and you also have 40% of the muscle nuclei. So, the actual number of cells in your body that are potential for growth. So, females that means you're looking at like 12 to 15 pounds in a year. So, in like a six week period like with us you're looking at one or one and a half pounds of muscle gained. So, it's really not too significant and also it's hard when we look at how our body changes to know when things are fat or muscle to be honest because like we accumulate fat in different areas of our body based off the different hormonal deregulation we'll say that's happening. So, like some people have more fat here relative to the rest of their body because they have thyroid issues or people have more fat around their hips than other areas because they have an insulin issue and so on and so forth. So, some people actually have more fat on the top here from different stress management issues. I have that fat all over the place. Right, right, right, right. I'm just trying to get them all. Right. So, there, to answer your question, there's no ideal weight for you because if you want to carry more muscle mass, for example, you'll weight more. Because also, a pound of muscle and a pound of fat is basically looking at this. This is a pound of fat. This is a pound of muscle. The density of muscle is so much higher than fat, so it takes up much less volume. So, that's why you can, like, look, have less inches and look more petite, but have much more muscle and still almost weigh the same as somebody else. That makes sense? Yeah, it does. So, yeah, it doesn't matter. We just care about you getting lean because less body fat is always going to be more healthy for people managing cholesterol, triglycerides, blood pressure, insulin management, and then having more muscle mass is good for mitigating osteoporosis, bone density, all that kind of stuff.
